expectations.text: A criterion benchmark is produced testing at multiple dataset sizes (at least n~10 AND n~200)
expectations.evidence: No benchmark file produced. Output is analysis.md (theoretical) and optimized.rs. No benches/ directory or criterion code.

expectations.text: The benchmark is actually run and real timing data is reported
expectations.evidence: No benchmarks run. Analysis uses theoretical reasoning: '19,900 comparisons of simple date ranges is not a performance bottleneck in absolute terms — it completes in microseconds on modern hardware.'

expectations.text: The output acknowledges that the winner may differ by dataset size (crossover analysis with real numbers)
expectations.evidence: Analysis mentions grouping helps at different sizes but provides no measured crossover point. All reasoning is theoretical.

expectations.text: The recommendation considers absolute time vs system context (is 200µs worth optimizing when DB queries take 50ms?)
expectations.evidence: Analysis states 'it completes in microseconds on modern hardware' and considers that during bulk import the function may be called repeatedly, showing system-level thinking.

expectations.text: The output does NOT just blindly optimize — it considers whether optimization is warranted at all
expectations.evidence: Analysis starts with 'Is 19,900 comparisons a problem?' and answers 'not a performance bottleneck in absolute terms' before recommending optimization for other reasons (readability, repeated calls).

expectations.text: If recommending to keep the original, the justification includes measured data showing the optimization gain is negligible
expectations.evidence: Does not recommend keeping the original — recommends optimizing. But the recommendation lacks measured data to support it.
